rationale_for_baseline_tier: Widespread but superficial acute mechanical/bacterial (frictional-occlusive) folliculitis after occlusive athletic wear and heavy sweating. The patient is systemically well and afebrile with the source's own red-flag screen negative (no deep tender/fluctuant nodules, no confluent cellulitic erythema, no lymphadenopathy, no systemic upset; Risk Level: Low), so this is neither an emergency (not T4/T5) nor an urgent same-day problem (not T3). It is telehealth/GP-manageable with an over-the-counter antibacterial wash, loose breathable cotton clothing, showering/changing immediately after exercise, not shaving the area, and an oral antihistamine for the itch — patient-performable self-care with a routine review pathway. The one prescription element (a topical antibacterial for the worst spots) is an adjunct obtainable at routine review, not an urgent requirement — so the baseline is self-care with routine GP review if not settling (T1) rather than an urgent tier, with prominent mandatory escalation edges for a deeper soft-tissue infection, systemic infection and the disseminated-HSV mimic. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED here is over-triage.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: One or more spots merging into a large, deeply painful, hot red lump or boil, or redness and heat spreading well beyond the spotty areas (with or without red streaks or tender groin lumps)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day in-person review (emergency department if it can't be arranged quickly)
escalation_edges.rationale: Indicates a deeper soft-tissue infection — a furuncle/carbuncle/abscess (which may need incision and drainage) or a spreading cellulitis (which needs antibiotics and in-person assessment) — a complication telehealth cannot palpate for.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If any of the spots turns into a big, deep, very painful hot lump or boil, or the redness and heat start spreading well beyond the spots, get seen in person the same day — that can mean a deeper skin infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever, chills or rigors, or feeling generally unwell in yourself, especially with the rash spreading rapidly
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day medical assessment; go to an emergency department (call 000 if rapidly unwell)
escalation_edges.rationale: Systemic features on top of a widespread staphylococcal skin process suggest invasive/systemic infection needing urgent in-person assessment and possible admission.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a fever or the shivers, or you start feeling generally unwell, get medical help the same day — go to an emergency department, and call 000 if you feel very unwell quickly.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rapidly spreading grouped painful blisters or uniform punched-out sores, or sores appearing in the mouth, eyes or genitals, or feeling unwell with the rash
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day in-person/emergency review (call 000 if rapidly unwell)
escalation_edges.rationale: Grouped painful blisters, punched-out erosions or mucosal sores raise disseminated herpes simplex / eczema herpeticum — a dermatological emergency needing antivirals and urgent assessment that telehealth cannot fully examine for.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the spots turn into rapidly spreading, grouped painful blisters or little punched-out sores, or you get sores in your mouth, eyes or genitals, or feel unwell, get seen the same day — this needs urgent assessment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Baseline: widespread but systemically-well superficial frictional/occlusive folliculitis with a negative red-flag screen
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Start the antibacterial wash, loose breathable clothing, post-exercise showering/changing and an antihistamine now, and arrange routine GP review within ~5-7 days if it has not significantly improved (including to obtain the topical antibiotic)
escalation_edges.rationale: The proportionate disposition once the deeper-infection, systemic and HSV mimics are excluded — begin patient-performable self-care immediately, with routine review if it does not settle; the prescription-only topical antibacterial is an adjunct obtainable at routine review.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Start an antibacterial wash, switch to loose cotton clothing, shower and change straight after exercise, don't shave the area, and use an antihistamine for the itch. If it hasn't clearly improved in about 5-7 days, book a routine GP review to check it and get a topical antibiotic.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: No improvement after about 5-7 days of treatment, or the eruption keeps recurring with exercise
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Prompt GP review within a few days if not settling or recurrent
escalation_edges.rationale: A folliculitis that fails to settle or recurs may need a bacterial swab (including MRSA), a topical/oral antibiotic, or reassessment of the diagnosis and triggers.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If it hasn't started improving within about 5-7 days, or it keeps coming back after exercise, book a GP review so they can swab it and adjust treatment.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Severe sleep deprivation and significant discomfort from the widespread itch
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Acknowledge the distress and sleep loss and offer the antihistamine and symptom measures, but do not let the degree of distress drive over-escalation of a systemically-well superficial folliculitis; confirm the patient can carry out the self-care and knows the specific red flags that warrant same-day review.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a common skin reaction — a folliculitis — where tight, sweaty running gear has irritated the hair follicles and let normal skin bacteria into hundreds of tiny surface spots. It's distressing and itchy, but not an emergency. Right now: use an antibacterial wash in the shower over the affected areas, switch to loose breathable cotton clothing, shower and change out of damp gear immediately after exercise, don't shave the area, and take an antihistamine for the itch. If it hasn't clearly improved in about 5-7 days, book a routine GP review to check it and get a topical antibiotic cream. You do not need an emergency department for the spots themselves.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Routine GP review within ~5-7 days if not settling (own GP or a telehealth prescriber for the topical antibiotic); 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) is available for advice
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If this is after hours you do not need an emergency department for the spots themselves — start the antibacterial wash, loose clothing and antihistamine and arrange a routine review if it isn't settling in about 5-7 days. Only seek same-day/emergency care if a red flag develops: a large deeply painful hot lump or spreading redness (same-day review), a fever or feeling generally unwell (same-day/ED), or rapidly spreading grouped painful blisters or mouth/eye sores (same-day review).
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Tell the reviewing clinician the eruption came up over ~24 hours the day after a long run in tight occlusive leggings with heavy sweating in humid conditions, as hundreds of monomorphic folliculocentric pustules on the thighs, calves and buttocks with normal intervening skin, intensely itchy and mildly tender, with no deep nodules, spreading cellulitis, lymphadenopathy, fever or systemic upset, no grouped vesicles or mucosal sores, no hot-tub exposure and no medications.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the widespread pustular eruption is a benign superficial frictional folliculitis or one of the mimics it must be screened against — a deeper soft-tissue infection (abscess/cellulitis), systemic infection, or disseminated herpes simplex / eczema herpeticum — which telehealth cannot palpate or fully examine.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Actively screen for deep painful lumps/spreading redness, fever/systemic upset, and grouped blisters/mucosal sores; treat a negative screen plus the classic folliculocentric morphology and mechanical trigger as a reason for self-care with routine review and explicit safety-netting — NOT as a reason to default to same-day ED / 000 when the patient is systemically well.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either dismissing a widespread pustular eruption with pure self-care and no safety-net for the deeper-infection or HSV mimics (under-action), or escalating a systemically-well patient with a negative red-flag screen straight to the emergency department / 000 (over-escalation).

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing a widespread pustular eruption as trivial with pure self-care and no safety-net for a deeper soft-tissue infection, systemic infection or disseminated HSV.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving abscess/cellulitis or disseminated herpes simplex progresses without anyone advising when to escalate, or a systemic infection is missed.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Providing self-care but with no mimic/deep-infection safety-net at all (baseline self-care is correct ONLY when paired with the mandatory red-flag escalation edges).

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a systemically-well patient with a negative red-flag screen to the emergency department or calling 000 for a common superficial frictional folliculitis — unnecessary ED load and patient distress/cost when self-care with routine review is proportionate (a prompt T2 review to obtain the topical antibiotic is an acceptable more-cautious disposition, not scored as over-triage).
